module: Sepsis and Septic Shock Part I: Introduction, definitions and recognition of sepsis

sections.Introduction: Sepsis is a life-threatening organ dysfunction characterized by a dysregulated host response to infection. It affects over 30 million people annually, with 11 million deaths worldwide in 2017 (Global Burden of Disease). Sepsis accounts for approximately one-third of all ICU admissions. Mortality ranges from 10–50% and survivors often suffer long-term physical, psychological, and cognitive disability. Three priorities govern management: (1) early recognition with rapid treatment initiation, (2) prevention and support of organ dysfunction, (3) rapid infection source control including adequate antimicrobials and surgical/interventional measures when indicated.
sections.Definition of Sepsis: Sepsis was first defined in 1992 as infection plus SIRS (≥2 of: HR >90/min, RR >20/min or mechanical ventilation, temperature <36°C or >38°C, WBC <4000 or >11,000/mm³). In 2001 (Sepsis-2), the PIRO staging concept was introduced (Predisposition, Insult, Response, Organ dysfunction), though it never gained widespread use. Sepsis-3 (Singer et al., 2016) redefines sepsis as 'a syndrome characterised by life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection.' Organ dysfunction is operationalised as an infection-related increase of ≥2 points in the SOFA score. 'Severe sepsis' is now obsolete — redundant since organ dysfunction is part of the core definition. Septic shock = sepsis + vasopressors to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg + serum lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation. Refractory septic shock = persistent elevated lactate + norepinephrine equivalent ≥0.5 mcg/kg/min.
sections.Assessment of Organ Dysfunction: The SOFA score assesses 6 organ systems: respiratory (PaO2/FiO2), coagulation (platelets), liver (bilirubin), cardiovascular (MAP and/or vasopressor dose), CNS (GCS), and renal (creatinine and/or urine output). Sepsis requires an infection-related increase of ≥2 SOFA points from baseline. The SOFA-2 score was published in 2025 incorporating modern interventions but has not yet entered routine clinical practice. The qSOFA was developed for non-ICU settings: ≥2 of (SBP ≤100 mmHg, RR ≥22/min, altered mentation) — no laboratory values required. However, SSC 2026 guidelines recommend AGAINST routine qSOFA use due to its low sensitivity. qSOFA is NOT intended for patients already in the ICU. A positive qSOFA prompts investigation for sepsis and possible ICU admission but does not confirm the diagnosis.
sections.Pathophysiology of Sepsis: Endothelial activation leads to glycocalyx damage, increased vascular permeability, and interstitial fluid leakage. Resulting oedema impairs tissue oxygenation by increasing the diffusion distance for oxygen from capillary to cell. Microcirculatory dysfunction: capillary occlusion by thrombi, activated leukocytes, and poorly deformable erythrocytes causes oxygenated blood to bypass occluded beds, failing to unload oxygen — producing tissue hypoxia despite adequate systemic delivery. Microvascular abnormalities can be assessed in research settings using sublingual imaging (sidestream dark-field microscopy) but no bedside tool is available clinically. Myocardial depression: poorly characterised depressant factors reduce contractility, yet cardiac output is characteristically INCREASED in adequately resuscitated sepsis (hyperdynamic circulation). Classical haemodynamic profile of resuscitated sepsis: tachycardia, warm extremities, increased CO, reduced SVR, peripheral oedema. Mitochondrial dysfunction: excess NO, CO, H2S, and reactive oxygen species cause direct mitochondrial damage and bioenergetic shutdown ('cellular hibernation') — possibly protective but manifest as multi-organ dysfunction.
sections.Diagnosing Infection: Recognising organ dysfunction is usually straightforward; confirming infection as its cause is harder. Initial steps: patient history, physical examination, laboratory values. A complete CT chest/abdomen with oral and IV contrast is justified if initial workup fails to identify the infection source. If no focus is identified, non-infectious causes of organ dysfunction should be considered. It is not uncommon for the infection site to remain unknown even in floridly septic patients.
sections.Use of Biomarkers: CRP: slow kinetics, non-specific (rises in pancreatitis, trauma, post-operative states) — less accurate than PCT for sepsis. Procalcitonin (PCT): moderate diagnostic accuracy for sepsis. SSC 2026 recommends using clinical evaluation ALONE rather than PCT + clinical evaluation to decide whether to start antibiotics. IL-6: fastest kinetics — peaks within 2 hours of infectious stimulus; well-established in paediatric critical care; limited adult evidence due to poor study quality. Biomarker panels may increase accuracy but are not widely available. Treatment decisions must never be based on a single biomarker alone.
sections.Finding the Causative Agent: At least 2 blood culture sets must be obtained before antibiotics, but must NOT unnecessarily delay antibiotic start. Positive blood cultures occur in only 30–40% of sepsis patients. Bacterial growth takes 2–3 days; results can be falsely negative or false positive. Molecular diagnostics (multiplex PCR, PCR/ESI-MS) provide results within a working day — a genuine advantage — but are expensive and lack large RCT evidence confirming clinical benefit. They are used in only a small number of specialist centres. The predominant organisms in ICU infections are gram-negative (67% of identified pathogens); community-acquired infections are most common (44%), then hospital-acquired (34.5%), then ICU-acquired (21.6%).

questions.question: A 65-year-old with community-acquired pneumonia has received 30 mL/kg crystalloid. Current observations: MAP 58 mmHg, norepinephrine 0.2 mcg/kg/min, serum lactate 2.8 mmol/L. Which condition best describes this clinical picture?
options: A: Severe sepsis with cardiovascular dysfunction
B: Septic shock (Sepsis-3 criteria)
C: Systemic inflammatory response syndrome with infectious aetiology
D: Sepsis with acute organ dysfunction pending vasopressor optimisation
E: Refractory septic shock
questions.explanation: Septic shock (Sepsis-3) requires vasopressors to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg AND serum lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation — both criteria are met here. 'Severe sepsis' is an obsolete term abandoned in Sepsis-3 because organ dysfunction is now inherent in the definition itself, and SIRS with an infectious cause is a broader, less specific category that does not capture the vasopressor requirement and lactate elevation seen here. Refractory septic shock requires a norepinephrine equivalent of ≥0.5 mcg/kg/min with persistent lactate elevation, which is not met at 0.2 mcg/kg/min, and sepsis with organ dysfunction pending vasopressor optimisation understates a picture that already meets the vasopressor-dependent shock definition.
questions.topic: septic shock Sepsis-3 vasopressor lactate MAP criteria

questions.question: A 72-year-old with a urinary tract infection develops new respiratory failure (PaO2/FiO2 220 mmHg) and rising creatinine from 150 to 230 µmol/L over 12 hours. According to Sepsis-3, what minimum change in SOFA score from baseline is required to diagnose sepsis?
options: A: An absolute SOFA score of ≥4 at any time
B: An increase of 3 or more points
C: An increase of 1 point
D: Any SOFA score ≥2 at the time of assessment
E: An infection-related increase of ≥2 points from baseline
questions.explanation: Sepsis-3 requires an infection-related increase of 2 or more SOFA points from baseline to operationalise organ dysfunction — not an absolute threshold and not a different point increment. An absolute SOFA score of 4 or more, or any SOFA score of 2 or more, both fail because they rely on an absolute value rather than a change from baseline; an increase of 3 or more points, or an increase of just 1 point, both use the right change-from-baseline concept but the wrong magnitude — the threshold is specifically 2 points, not 3 or 1. This distinction is clinically important: a patient with chronic kidney disease may have a baseline SOFA of 3, but only qualifies for sepsis if a new increase of 2 or more points occurs.
questions.topic: SOFA increase sepsis diagnosis Sepsis-3 organ dysfunction baseline

questions.question: A 55-year-old presents to the emergency department with fever and cough. RR is 24/min, BP 110/70 mmHg, she is fully oriented. A junior doctor proposes using qSOFA to screen for sepsis risk. Which statement best reflects current SSC 2026 guidance on qSOFA?
options: A: Current SSC 2026 guidelines recommend against routine qSOFA use due to its low sensitivity
B: qSOFA should be calculated in all ED patients with suspected infection as the primary screening tool
C: A negative qSOFA reliably excludes sepsis in non-ICU patients
D: qSOFA is recommended for ICU patients because it requires no laboratory testing
E: A qSOFA score of ≥2 confirms sepsis and mandates immediate ICU admission
questions.explanation: SSC 2026 explicitly recommends against using qSOFA as a routine screening tool because of its low sensitivity — it misses a substantial proportion of sepsis cases, which is dangerous in a condition where early recognition is critical; this also means it should not be relied on as the primary screening tool for all ED patients with suspected infection. qSOFA is not intended for ICU patients, having been designed and validated for non-ICU settings, so recommending it there because it avoids laboratory testing misunderstands its intended use. A positive qSOFA score does not confirm sepsis nor mandate immediate ICU admission — it only prompts further investigation — and conversely, a negative qSOFA does not reliably exclude sepsis either.
questions.topic: qSOFA SSC 2026 low sensitivity recommendation against use

questions.question: A trainee documents 'SIRS criteria met (HR 102, temperature 38.5°C, WBC 13,200/mm³, RR 18/min) — patient has sepsis' for a patient on day 2 post-laparotomy. The attending disagrees. What is the most important limitation of using SIRS criteria to diagnose sepsis in this patient?
options: A: Only 2 SIRS criteria are met; at least 3 are required for sepsis
B: SIRS criteria must be accompanied by haemodynamic instability to indicate sepsis
C: SIRS is poorly specific and is present in non-infectious conditions such as post-operative inflammation
D: The temperature threshold for SIRS is ≥39.0°C, not 38.5°C
E: Sepsis diagnosis requires microbiological confirmation of infection before it can be established
questions.explanation: SIRS requires 2 or more of 4 criteria, and in this patient heart rate, temperature and white cell count together already meet 3 criteria, so a claim that only 2 are met (with 3 required) is factually incorrect on both counts. The critical failure of SIRS is poor specificity: it is present in almost all critically ill patients from non-infectious causes including post-operative states, burns, and pancreatitis — which is precisely why Sepsis-3 replaced SIRS with SOFA-based organ dysfunction, and why SIRS criteria are not validated by simply adding a haemodynamic-instability requirement. A specific 39.0°C temperature threshold is not correct either — the SIRS temperature criterion is met above 38°C. Microbiological confirmation is desirable but not required for a sepsis diagnosis — sepsis remains a clinical diagnosis.
questions.topic: SIRS specificity limitations post-operative Sepsis-3 non-infectious

questions.question: An 80-year-old patient with septic shock from urosepsis has received adequate fluid resuscitation. Invasive haemodynamic monitoring is performed. Which profile is most consistent with adequately resuscitated septic shock?
options: A: Tachycardia, cold extremities, normal cardiac output, normal systemic vascular resistance
B: Normal heart rate, peripheral oedema, increased systemic vascular resistance, normal cardiac output
C: Bradycardia, warm extremities, low cardiac output, reduced systemic vascular resistance
D: Tachycardia, cold extremities, low cardiac output, increased systemic vascular resistance
E: Tachycardia, warm extremities, increased cardiac output, reduced systemic vascular resistance
questions.explanation: Resuscitated septic shock is characteristically hyperdynamic: tachycardia, warm extremities from profound vasodilation, increased cardiac output, and reduced systemic vascular resistance. Although sepsis-induced myocardial depression occurs, cardiac output is typically maintained or increased when fluid resuscitation is adequate. Cold extremities with increased systemic vascular resistance and low cardiac output instead describes cardiogenic or unresuscitated distributive shock — the classic near-miss distractor — while the remaining profiles combine findings (normal or increased SVR with normal cardiac output, or bradycardia with vasodilation and low output) that do not match the hyperdynamic, adequately resuscitated picture described here.
questions.topic: resuscitated septic shock hyperdynamic cardiac output SVR warm extremities

questions.question: A 68-year-old develops fever, hypotension, and confusion on the surgical ward on day 4 post-laparotomy. Sepsis is suspected and broad-spectrum antibiotics are prepared. What is the most appropriate approach to blood culture sampling?
options: A: Obtain at least two blood culture sets before starting antibiotics, without unnecessarily delaying treatment
B: Obtain a single blood culture set immediately before antibiotics
C: Skip cultures entirely and start antibiotics — cultures are rarely positive in hospital-acquired sepsis
D: Draw blood cultures only from the central venous catheter to maximise pathogen yield
E: Delay antibiotics until all culture sets show microbial growth to ensure targeted therapy
questions.explanation: At least two blood culture sets, not just one, must be drawn before starting antibiotics, but sampling must never unnecessarily delay antimicrobial therapy. Skipping cultures entirely is not appropriate either, even though culture yield in sepsis is imperfect — cultures still guide de-escalation and targeted therapy when they are positive. Drawing cultures only from the central venous catheter increases contamination rates; peripheral blood cultures should be included alongside or instead. Delaying antibiotics until all culture sets show microbial growth is dangerous, since growth takes 2-3 days and antimicrobial therapy should not be withheld while awaiting results. Even with correct technique, only 30-40% of cultures are positive in sepsis.
questions.topic: blood cultures two sets antibiotic timing peripheral CVC

questions.question: Two blood culture sets are drawn at antibiotic initiation for a 57-year-old with clinical sepsis. All cultures return negative at 72 hours. The microbiologist states this is not unexpected. Which statement best explains this finding?
options: A: Blood cultures are nearly always positive within 24 hours in true bacteraemia
B: Negative cultures at 72 hours indicate the antibiotic selection was inappropriate
C: Positive blood cultures are found in only 30–40% of sepsis patients even with correct sampling technique
D: The two-set protocol is insufficient; at least four sets are required for acceptable sensitivity
E: Blood cultures have acceptable sensitivity only for gram-positive organisms in ICU-acquired sepsis
questions.explanation: Positive blood cultures occur in only 30-40% of patients with sepsis regardless of technique or antibiotic timing — a well-recognised limitation, not a sign of inadequate sampling or an insufficient number of culture sets. Growth typically takes 2-3 days, so blood cultures are not nearly always positive within 24 hours even in true bacteraemia, and false negatives are common. Negative cultures at 72 hours should not be read as evidence that the antibiotic selection was inappropriate, nor should they lead to discontinuing therapy when the clinical picture of sepsis remains clear. Gram-negative bacteria actually predominate, accounting for 67% of identified ICU pathogens, so blood culture sensitivity is not limited to gram-positive organisms.
questions.topic: blood culture sensitivity 30-40% false negative gram-negative sepsis

questions.question: A patient in the ICU develops new multi-organ dysfunction and the team wants to use a biomarker to differentiate septic from non-infectious causes. Which statement best explains why procalcitonin is preferred over CRP for this purpose?
options: A: Procalcitonin, like CRP, is synthesised by the liver as an acute-phase reactant, but with faster induction kinetics
B: SSC 2026 guidelines recommend adding procalcitonin to clinical evaluation when deciding to initiate antibiotics
C: Procalcitonin normalises more slowly than CRP, enabling prolonged monitoring of treatment response
D: CRP lacks specificity for infection, rising in non-infectious states as well, whereas procalcitonin is more specific for sepsis
E: Procalcitonin peaks within 2 hours of infectious stimulus, providing a faster signal than CRP
questions.explanation: CRP's primary limitation is poor specificity — it rises in non-infectious conditions including pancreatitis, trauma, and post-surgical states — whereas procalcitonin has better specificity for infection, though its sensitivity is moderate. Procalcitonin is not simply a faster-kinetics version of the same acute-phase pathway as CRP; the two are synthesised via different mechanisms. SSC 2026 actually recommends clinical evaluation alone over adding procalcitonin to clinical evaluation when deciding whether to initiate antibiotics, so the guidelines do not endorse routinely adding PCT to that decision. Its value lies in specificity for infection rather than in normalising more slowly than CRP for monitoring purposes. The distinctive 2-hour peak after an infectious stimulus describes IL-6 kinetics, not procalcitonin, which peaks later.
questions.topic: procalcitonin CRP specificity non-infectious inflammation SSC 2026

questions.question: A 43-year-old is admitted to ICU with suspected septic shock. The team wants to identify an infectious stimulus at the earliest possible time point using biomarkers. Which biomarker reaches peak plasma levels within 2 hours of infection onset?
options: A: Procalcitonin (PCT)
B: C-reactive protein (CRP)
C: Fibrinogen
D: Interleukin-6 (IL-6)
E: Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH)
questions.explanation: IL-6 is the fastest biomarker, peaking within 2 hours of infectious stimulus. CRP has slow kinetics (peak ~24–48 hours). PCT peaks at approximately 24 hours. Despite its rapid kinetics, IL-6 has limited adult evidence due to low-quality studies and is primarily used in paediatric critical care. Serum lactate reflects tissue hypoperfusion rather than the infectious stimulus itself.
questions.topic: IL-6 fastest biomarker 2 hours infection onset CRP PCT

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the three qSOFA criteria?
options: A: Respiratory rate ≥22 breaths/min
B: Temperature <36°C or >38°C
C: Altered mentation
D: Systolic blood pressure ≤100 mmHg
E: qSOFA cannot be scored without laboratory confirmation of organ dysfunction
questions.explanation: qSOFA has exactly three criteria: RR ≥22/min, altered mentation, and SBP ≤100 mmHg. Temperature abnormality is NOT part of qSOFA — it was a component of SIRS criteria, making it the archetypal near-miss distractor for this topic. qSOFA was specifically designed to exclude temperature and all laboratory values to enable rapid non-ICU bedside screening.
questions.topic: qSOFA NOT criteria temperature SIRS near-miss three criteria

questions.question: A 70-year-old with septic shock from pneumonia develops widespread pitting oedema and rising fluid requirements despite adequate cardiac output and MAP. Which mechanism best explains the pathogenesis of tissue oedema in this patient?
options: A: Raised oncotic pressure from acute-phase protein synthesis causing intravascular-to-interstitial fluid shifts
B: Capillary occlusion by thrombi increasing upstream hydrostatic pressure
C: Glycocalyx damage and increased endothelial permeability causing fluid to leak into the interstitium
D: Impaired lymphatic drainage secondary to hyperdynamic circulation and tachycardia
E: Renin-angiotensin-aldosterone activation causing pathological sodium and water retention
questions.explanation: Endothelial cell activation in sepsis causes glycocalyx damage, increasing vascular permeability so fluid leaks from the intravascular space into the interstitium. This oedema not only worsens effective hypovolaemia but also impairs tissue oxygenation by increasing the diffusion distance for oxygen. Raised oncotic pressure from acute-phase protein synthesis would be expected to retain fluid within the vasculature, not cause it to leak into the interstitium, so this does not explain the oedema seen here. Capillary occlusion by thrombi is a feature of microcirculatory dysfunction but does not primarily raise upstream hydrostatic pressure enough to explain widespread oedema, impaired lymphatic drainage is not a recognised consequence of a hyperdynamic circulation, and renin-angiotensin-aldosterone activation, which promotes sodium and water retention, is a secondary compensatory response rather than the primary driver of capillary leak.
questions.topic: glycocalyx endothelial permeability oedema interstitial diffusion oxygen

questions.question: A patient with septic shock has MAP 70 mmHg, cardiac output 7 L/min, and SaO2 99%, yet serum lactate is 4.2 mmol/L with evidence of progressive organ failure. Which mechanism best explains the discrepancy between normal systemic oxygen delivery and the tissue oxygen deficit?
options: A: Systemic oxygen delivery is actually inadequate despite the apparently normal haemodynamic parameters
B: Glycocalyx damage impairs haemoglobin oxygen loading at the alveolar-capillary interface
C: Mitochondrial dysfunction caused by excess reactive oxygen species prevents cellular oxygen utilization
D: Elevated cardiac output reduces capillary transit time, preventing sufficient oxygen unloading per red cell
E: Microcirculatory occlusion diverts oxygenated blood past hypoperfused tissue beds, impairing local oxygen delivery
questions.explanation: The classic sepsis microcirculatory lesion is distributive flow failure: capillary occlusion by thrombi, activated leukocytes, and poorly deformable erythrocytes redirects blood through patent capillaries, leaving adjacent hypoperfused cells hypoxic and generating lactate despite normal systemic delivery parameters. Systemic oxygen delivery is not in fact inadequate here — the haemodynamic parameters given are normal — so the discrepancy lies downstream of delivery rather than in delivery itself. Glycocalyx damage affects vascular permeability rather than haemoglobin oxygen loading at the alveolar-capillary interface, and mitochondrial dysfunction from excess reactive oxygen species also contributes to cytopathic hypoxia in sepsis, but the pattern of normal systemic parameters with tissue failure most specifically points to microvascular shunting rather than a cellular utilisation defect alone. Elevated cardiac output reducing capillary transit time is not the mechanism driving the tissue oxygen deficit described here.
questions.topic: microcirculation capillary occlusion tissue hypoxia lactate systemic delivery

questions.question: In a large international point-prevalence study of ICU patients with infection, which anatomical focus accounted for the largest proportion of infections?
options: A: Respiratory tract infections (pneumonia and tracheobronchitis)
B: Skin and soft tissue infections
C: Bloodstream infections (bacteraemia/fungaemia)
D: Urinary tract infections, including catheter-associated UTI
E: Abdominal infections, including intra-abdominal abscess and peritonitis
questions.explanation: The respiratory tract accounts for 60.1% of ICU infections — by far the most common focus, driven predominantly by pneumonia including ventilator-associated pneumonia. The abdomen is second at 18.3%, followed by bloodstream infections at 15.2%. This distribution directly informs empirical antibiotic selection and underscores the importance of respiratory specimen collection in suspected sepsis of unknown source.
questions.topic: ICU infection foci respiratory tract most common epidemiology

questions.question: Which of the following parameters is NOT assessed by the SOFA score?
options: A: Glasgow Coma Scale score
B: PaO2/FiO2 ratio
C: Platelet count
D: Serum bilirubin
E: Serum procalcitonin
questions.explanation: SOFA assesses 6 organ systems: respiratory (PaO2/FiO2), coagulation (platelets), liver (bilirubin), cardiovascular (MAP and/or vasopressor dose), CNS (Glasgow Coma Scale), and renal (creatinine and/or urine output). Procalcitonin is a biomarker of infection/inflammation and is not a SOFA component — it is the classic near-miss here because PCT is routinely used alongside SOFA in sepsis management, causing confusion. The Glasgow Coma Scale score, PaO2/FiO2 ratio, platelet count, and serum bilirubin are all genuine SOFA components.
questions.topic: SOFA score components procalcitonin NOT included six systems

questions.question: An ICU attending proposes using multiplex PCR for rapid pathogen identification in a patient with septic shock. A colleague asks why this is not standard practice. Which statement best explains the current limited clinical uptake of molecular diagnostics in sepsis?
options: A: Multiplex PCR results take 48–72 hours, offering no meaningful advantage over conventional blood cultures
B: Molecular PCR testing is contraindicated once patients have received any antibiotic doses
C: Cost and the absence of confirmed outcome benefit in large randomised trials limit routine adoption
D: PCR requires an antibiotic-free sampling window of at least 72 hours before blood collection
E: Current PCR platforms cannot reliably detect gram-negative organisms, which predominate in sepsis
questions.explanation: Multiplex PCR provides results within a working day — a genuine advantage over cultures (2–3 days). The primary barrier to routine adoption is cost combined with the absence of large RCT evidence confirming improved patient outcomes. Gram-negative organisms are detectable. PCR is not contraindicated in antibiotic-treated patients. The technology is promising but remains limited to specialist centres.
questions.topic: multiplex PCR molecular diagnostics cost RCT evidence limitation

questions.question: Regarding the Sepsis-3 (2016) consensus definition of sepsis:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The term 'severe sepsis' remains a valid clinical classification for patients with sepsis and associated organ failure.

statements.label: B
statements.text: The definition was developed by a panel of international experts and published in 2016.

statements.label: C
statements.text: The definition requires ≥2 SIRS criteria in addition to suspected infection.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Organ dysfunction is operationalised by an infection-related SOFA score increase of ≥2 points from baseline.

questions.explanation: Sepsis-3 replaced SIRS with SOFA-based organ dysfunction (an infection-related increase of 2 or more points from baseline) — SIRS is no longer part of the definition, so the claim that the definition still requires 2 or more SIRS criteria is false. 'Severe sepsis' was abolished as a term because organ dysfunction is now inherent in the core sepsis definition, making it redundant, so it does not remain a valid separate clinical classification. The Sepsis-3 taskforce comprised international experts and the definition was published in 2016, as described.
questions.topic: Sepsis-3 definition SOFA severe sepsis SIRS obsolete 2016

questions.question: Regarding the Sepsis-3 diagnostic criteria for septic shock:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Refractory septic shock is defined by norepinephrine equivalent ≥0.25 mcg/kg/min with persistent lactate elevation.

statements.label: B
statements.text: A vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg is a required diagnostic criterion.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Septic shock can be diagnosed in the absence of evidence of organ dysfunction.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Serum lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation is a required diagnostic criterion.

questions.explanation: Septic shock requires both a vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP of 65 mmHg or more, and a serum lactate above 2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation — both genuine diagnostic criteria. Since septic shock is a subset of sepsis, organ dysfunction is always implied, so the claim that septic shock can be diagnosed in the absence of organ dysfunction is false. Refractory septic shock requires a norepinephrine equivalent of 0.5 mcg/kg/min or more with persistent lactate elevation — not 0.25, which is a common error that halves the correct threshold.
questions.topic: septic shock criteria vasopressor lactate MAP refractory threshold

questions.question: Regarding the qSOFA score and its clinical application:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The three qSOFA criteria are: RR ≥22/min, altered mentation, and SBP ≤100 mmHg.

statements.label: B
statements.text: The Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2026 guidelines recommend against routine qSOFA use due to its low sensitivity.

statements.label: C
statements.text: A qSOFA score of ≥2 confirms the diagnosis of sepsis.

statements.label: D
statements.text: qSOFA is recommended for use in ICU patients as a rapid, laboratory-free organ dysfunction tool.

questions.explanation: qSOFA has three criteria: respiratory rate 22/min or more, altered mentation, and systolic blood pressure 100 mmHg or below. SSC 2026 recommends against routine qSOFA use specifically because of its low sensitivity, even in its intended non-ICU domain. The claim that a qSOFA score of 2 or more confirms the diagnosis of sepsis is false — a raised qSOFA prompts further investigation and possible escalation but does not itself confirm sepsis; SOFA remains the reference standard for organ dysfunction. qSOFA is also not recommended for use in ICU patients as a rapid, laboratory-free tool — it was designed and validated for non-ICU settings.
questions.topic: qSOFA criteria SSC 2026 sensitivity ICU recommendation confirmation

questions.question: Regarding the Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (SOFA) score:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Any absolute SOFA score ≥2 satisfies the Sepsis-3 organ dysfunction criterion regardless of the patient's baseline.

statements.label: B
statements.text: The SOFA-2 score was published in 2025 to incorporate modern interventions but has not yet entered routine clinical practice.

statements.label: C
statements.text: The SOFA score encompasses respiratory, cardiovascular, renal, hepatic, coagulation, and neurological function.

statements.label: D
statements.text: A persistently rising SOFA score during ICU admission is associated with improved survival.

questions.explanation: The SOFA score encompasses respiratory, cardiovascular, renal, hepatic, coagulation, and neurological function — all 6 organ systems as described. Sepsis-3 requires a change of 2 or more points from baseline, not an absolute threshold — a patient with a chronic baseline SOFA of 3 does not meet the sepsis organ-dysfunction criterion until a new increment of 2 or more is added, so the claim that any absolute SOFA score of 2 or more satisfies the criterion regardless of baseline is false. SOFA-2 exists, having been published in 2025, but has not yet entered routine clinical practice, as described. A persistently rising SOFA score during ICU admission is associated with higher mortality, not improved survival — a common reversal trap.
questions.topic: SOFA components absolute vs increase SOFA-2 prognosis rising

questions.question: Regarding biomarkers in the diagnosis and management of sepsis:

statements.label: A
statements.text: IL-6 reaches peak plasma levels within 2 hours of infectious stimulus, faster than both CRP and procalcitonin.

statements.label: B
statements.text: SSC 2026 recommends using procalcitonin combined with clinical evaluation, rather than clinical evaluation alone, when deciding to initiate antibiotics.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Procalcitonin is more specific for sepsis than CRP because CRP also rises markedly in non-infectious inflammatory conditions.

statements.label: D
statements.text: A single normal procalcitonin result reliably excludes a diagnosis of sepsis.

questions.explanation: IL-6 reaches peak plasma levels within 2 hours of an infectious stimulus, faster than both CRP and procalcitonin. Procalcitonin is more specific for sepsis than CRP, since CRP also rises markedly in non-infectious inflammatory conditions. SSC 2026 actually recommends clinical evaluation alone, not procalcitonin combined with clinical evaluation, when deciding to initiate antibiotics — a high-yield reversal trap. Procalcitonin has only moderate diagnostic accuracy, so a single normal result does not reliably exclude a diagnosis of sepsis.
questions.topic: PCT CRP IL-6 kinetics SSC 2026 antibiotic initiation single biomarker

questions.question: Regarding blood culture collection in suspected sepsis:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Blood culture collection should not unnecessarily delay the start of antibiotic therapy.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Bacterial colonies in standard blood culture systems typically become detectable within 6–8 hours.

statements.label: C
statements.text: A minimum of two blood culture sets should be obtained before initiating antimicrobial therapy.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Positive blood cultures are found in approximately 70–80% of patients with confirmed sepsis.

questions.explanation: Blood culture collection should not unnecessarily delay the start of antibiotic therapy, and a minimum of two blood culture sets should be obtained before initiating antimicrobial therapy — both correct. The claim that positive blood cultures are found in approximately 70-80% of patients with confirmed sepsis is false — positive cultures occur in only 30-40% of sepsis patients, and the higher figure is a common optimistic misconception. The claim that bacterial colonies typically become detectable within 6-8 hours is also false — standard cultures take 2-3 days for growth; only molecular methods such as multiplex PCR can provide results within a working day.
questions.topic: blood cultures two sets 30-40% positivity growth time delay

questions.question: Regarding endothelial and microvascular dysfunction in sepsis:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Glycocalyx damage increases capillary permeability, leading to interstitial fluid leakage and peripheral oedema.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Interstitial oedema impairs tissue oxygenation by increasing the diffusion distance from capillary to cell.

statements.label: C
statements.text: In sepsis, glycocalyx damage primarily increases arterial wall permeability rather than capillary permeability.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Endothelial dysfunction and glycocalyx damage can be routinely quantified at the ICU bedside using standard monitoring equipment.

questions.explanation: Glycocalyx damage increases capillary, not arterial, permeability, leading to interstitial fluid leakage and peripheral oedema — so the claim that it primarily increases arterial wall permeability is false. This resulting interstitial oedema impairs tissue oxygenation by increasing the diffusion distance from capillary to cell. The claim that endothelial dysfunction and glycocalyx damage can be routinely quantified at the ICU bedside using standard monitoring equipment is also false — there is currently no such bedside tool; sublingual imaging techniques that can assess this are only available in research settings.
questions.topic: glycocalyx capillary permeability oedema diffusion bedside monitoring

questions.question: Regarding the historical SIRS-based sepsis definitions (Sepsis-1 and Sepsis-2):

statements.label: A
statements.text: Hypothermia (temperature <36°C) qualifies as a temperature criterion in SIRS and can be a manifestation of sepsis.

statements.label: B
statements.text: The 2001 Sepsis-2 update introduced the PIRO staging concept (Predisposition, Insult, Response, Organ dysfunction).

statements.label: C
statements.text: SIRS requires ≥2 of the following: HR >90/min, RR >20/min, temperature <36°C or >38°C, WBC <4,000 or >11,000/mm³.

statements.label: D
statements.text: SIRS criteria were removed from the sepsis definition primarily because of their poor sensitivity for detecting infection.

questions.explanation: Hypothermia (temperature below 36°C) qualifies as a temperature criterion in SIRS and can be a manifestation of sepsis, and SIRS itself requires 2 or more of: heart rate above 90/min, respiratory rate above 20/min, temperature below 36°C or above 38°C, and white cell count below 4,000 or above 11,000/mm³ — both accurately described. The 2001 Sepsis-2 update did introduce the PIRO staging concept (Predisposition, Insult, Response, Organ dysfunction), though it never gained widespread clinical adoption. The claim that SIRS criteria were removed from the sepsis definition primarily because of poor sensitivity for detecting infection is false — SIRS was abandoned for poor specificity, since it is present in many non-infectious conditions; reversing sensitivity and specificity here is the archetypal trap.
questions.topic: SIRS criteria specificity sensitivity PIRO hypothermia Sepsis-2

questions.question: Regarding the global epidemiology of sepsis and infection in ICU patients:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The respiratory tract is the most commonly identified focus of infection in ICU patients.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Sepsis accounts for approximately one-third of all ICU admissions worldwide.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Hospital-acquired infections represent the largest single category of ICU infections, exceeding community-acquired infections in prevalence.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Among identified pathogens in ICU infections, gram-negative organisms account for the majority.

questions.explanation: The respiratory tract is indeed the most commonly identified focus of infection in ICU patients, accounting for about 60.1% of cases, and sepsis drives approximately one-third of all ICU admissions worldwide — both accurately described. Among identified pathogens in ICU infections, gram-negative organisms do account for the majority, at around 67%. The claim that hospital-acquired infections represent the largest single category, exceeding community-acquired infections, is false — community-acquired infections are actually the largest single category at about 44%, ahead of hospital-acquired (34.5%) and ICU-acquired (21.6%) infections.
questions.topic: sepsis epidemiology ICU gram-negative community-acquired respiratory foci

questions.question: Regarding microcirculatory and mitochondrial dysfunction in sepsis:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Mitochondrial dysfunction in sepsis involves generation of excess reactive oxygen and nitrogen species causing direct damage to mitochondrial structures.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Microvascular abnormalities in sepsis can be assessed in research settings using sublingual imaging techniques such as sidestream dark-field microscopy.

statements.label: C
statements.text: In adequately resuscitated septic shock, cardiac output is characteristically reduced due to sepsis-induced myocardial depression.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Capillary occlusion by thrombi and poorly deformable red cells can cause tissue hypoxia despite adequate systemic oxygen delivery parameters.

questions.explanation: Mitochondrial dysfunction in sepsis does involve the generation of excess reactive oxygen and nitrogen species, including NO, CO, and H2S, causing direct damage to mitochondrial structures. Microvascular abnormalities in sepsis can indeed be assessed in research settings using sublingual imaging techniques such as sidestream dark-field microscopy, though this is not yet available for routine clinical use. The claim that cardiac output is characteristically reduced in adequately resuscitated septic shock due to myocardial depression is false and reversed — although myocardial depression does occur, cardiac output is characteristically increased in adequately resuscitated sepsis, reflecting a hyperdynamic circulation. Capillary occlusion by thrombi and poorly deformable red cells can indeed cause tissue hypoxia despite adequate systemic oxygen delivery parameters, consistent with distributive microcirculatory flow failure.
questions.topic: microcirculation capillary occlusion cardiac output hyperdynamic sublingual imaging
